top_clauses.title: Combined shear force, axial force and bending moment

response.answer:
For **IPE400** in **S355** steel, the design shear resistance is **VRd = 865.95 kN**. This follows EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 5.5.2(1) (Classification of cross-sections) with supporting classification guidance from Cl. Table 5.2 (Width-to-thickness limits).
Section Classification detailed results **Inputs** | Input | Value | | --- | --- | | section name | IPE400 | | h (mm) | 400 mm | | b (mm) | 180 mm | | tw (mm) | 8.60 mm | | tf (mm) | 13.50 mm | | fy mpa | 355 MPa | **Outputs** | Output | Value | | --- | --- | | web class | **4** | | flange class | **1** | | governing class | **4** | **Notes** - This MVP uses simplified threshold values aligned to EC3 structure for demonstrative chaining.
Member Resistance detailed results **Inputs** | Input | Value | | --- | --- | | section name | IPE400 | | steel grade | S355 | | fy mpa | 355 MPa | | section class | 4 | | γM0 | 1 | | w used (cm³) | 1156 cm³ | | area (cm²) | 84.50 cm² | | av z (cm²) | 42.25 cm² | **Outputs** | Output | Value | | --- | --- | | MRd (kNm) | **410.38 kNm** | | NRd (kN) | **2999.75 kN** | | VRd (kN) | **865.95 kN** | | bending formula basis | **MRd = W * fy / γM0** | | axial formula basis | **NRd = A * fy / γM0** | | shear formula basis | **VRd = A_v * fy / (sqrt(3) * γM0)** | **Notes** - Member buckling and lateral torsional buckling are outside this MVP placeholder calculator.
Assumptions used - Assumed steel grade S355 as it was not specified. - Assumed partial safety factor γM0 = 1.0 as per standard Eurocode 3 defaults.
--- **References:** 1. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 5.5.2(1) — Classification of cross-sections 2. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. Table 5.2 — Width-to-thickness limits 3. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 6.2.5(1) — Bending resistance 4. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 6.2.4(1) — Compression resistance 5. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 6.2.6(1) — Shear resistance

response.answer:
This follows EN 1993-1-3, Ref. Table 10.5 (Reaction force at support for simply supported beam) with supporting reference to EN 1993-1-6, Ref. B.5.2 (Local distributed load, simply supported boundary). This follows EN 1993-1-3, Ref. Table 10.5 (Reaction force at support for simply supported beam) with supporting reference to EN 1993-1-6, Ref. B.5.2 (Local distributed load, simply supported boundary). --- **References:**
1. EN 1993-1-3, Ref. Table 10.5 — Reaction force at support for simply supported beam

| Beam and loading | Reaction force on bottom flange $R_1$ | Reaction force on top flange $R_2$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Z-beam, gravity loading | $(1-\xi)k_hq_{Ed}L/2$ | $(1+\xi)k_hq_{Ed}L/2$ |
| Z-beam, uplift loading | $-(1-\xi)k_hq_{Ed}L/2$ | $-(1+\xi)k_hq_{Ed}L/2$ |
| C-beam, gravity loading | $(1-\xi)k_hq_{Ed}L/2$ | $-(1-\xi)k_hq_{Ed}L/2$ |
| C-beam, uplift loading | $-(1-\xi)k_hq_{Ed}L/2$ | $(1-\xi)k_hq_{Ed}L/2$ |

(3) The factor $\zeta$ may be taken as $\zeta = 1 - \sqrt[3]{\kappa_R^2}$ where $\kappa_R$ is the correction factor given in Table 10.1, and the factor $\xi$ may be taken as $\xi = 1,5\zeta$.

2. EN 1993-1-6, Ref. B.5.2 — Local distributed load, simply supported boundary

Uniform pressure $p_n$ on circular patch of radius $b$
$$F = p_n \pi b^2$$

with
$$K = 1,0 + 1,10 \frac{b}{r} + 1,15 \left( \frac{b}{r} \right)^4 \text{ or } K = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{b}{t}$$
whichever is the lesser

3. EN 1993-1-6, Ref. B.5.1 — Uniform load, simply supported boundary

$$p_{n,R} = 1,625 \left( \frac{t}{r} \right)^2 fy$$

4. EN 1993-1-6, Ref. C.6.1 — Plate with simply supported boundary: uniform load

$$w = 0.696 \frac{p_n r^4}{Et^3}$$

$$\text{max. } \sigma_{xb} = 1.238 p_n \left( \frac{r}{t} \right)^2$$

$$\text{max. } \sigma_{\theta b} = 1.238 p_n \left( \frac{r}{t} \right)^2$$

$$p_{n,y} = 0.808 \left( \frac{t}{r} \right)^2 fy$$

5. EN 1993-1-6, Ref. C.6.2 — Plate with local distributed load: simply supported boundary

Uniform pressure $p_n$ on circular patch of radius $b$
$$F = p_n \pi b^2$$

$$b < 0.2 r$$

$$w = 0.606 \frac{Fr^2}{Et^3}$$

$$\text{max. } \sigma_{xb} = \text{max. } \sigma_{\theta b} = 0.621 \frac{F}{t^2} \left( \ln \frac{b}{r} + 0.769 \right)$$

$$F_y = 1.611 \frac{t^2}{\left( \ln \frac{b}{r} + 0.769 \right) fy}$$

6. EN 1993-1-3, Ref. A.2.2 — Single span test

(1) A test set-up equivalent to that shown in figure A.2 may be used to determine the midspan moment resistance (in the absence of shear force) and the effective flexural stiffness.

(2) The span should be chosen such that the test results represent the moment resistance of the sheet.

(3) The moment resistance should be determined from the test result.

(4) The flexural stiffness should be determined from a plot of the load-deflection behaviour.

--- **References:**
1. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 5.5.2(1) — Classification of cross-sections

(1) Four classes of cross-sections are defined, as follows:

- Class 1 cross-sections are those which can form a plastic hinge with the rotation capacity required from plastic analysis without reduction of the resistance.

- Class 2 cross-sections are those which can develop their plastic moment resistance, but have limited rotation capacity because of local buckling.

- Class 3 cross-sections are those in which the stress in the extreme compression fibre of the steel member assuming an elastic distribution of stresses can reach the yield strength, but local buckling is liable to prevent development of the plastic moment resistance.

- Class 4 cross-sections are those in which local buckling will occur before the attainment of yield stress in one or more parts of the cross-section.

(2) In Class 4 cross sections effective widths may be used to make the necessary allowances for reductions in resistance due to the effects of local buckling, see EN 1993-1-5, 4.4 AC2.

(3) The classification of a cross-section depends on the width to thickness ratio of the parts subject to compression.

(4) Compression parts include every part of a cross-section which is either totally or partially in compression under the load combination considered.

(5) The various compression parts in a cross-section (such as a web or flange) can, in general, be in different classes.

(6) A cross-section is cla …

2. EN 1993-1-1, Table 5.2 — Width-to-thickness limits

Angles

Refer also to “Outstand flanges” (see sheet 2 of 3)

Does not apply to angles in continuous contact with other components

Class

Section in compression

Stress distribution across section (compression positive)

$$h/t \leq 15\varepsilon$$ and $$\frac{b+h}{2t} \leq 11,5\varepsilon$$

Tubular sections

Class

Section in bending and/or compression

$$d/t \leq 50\varepsilon^2$$

$$d/t \leq 70\varepsilon^2$$

$$d/t \leq 90\varepsilon^2$$

NOTE For $d/t > 90\varepsilon^2$ see EN 1993-1-6.

$$\varepsilon = \sqrt{235/fy}$$

$$fy$$ 235 275 355 420 460

$$\varepsilon$$ 1,00 0,92 0,81 0,75 0,71

$$\varepsilon^2$$ 1,00 0,85 0,66 0,56 0,51

3. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 6.2.5(1) — Bending resistance

(1)P The design value of the bending moment $M_{Ed}$ at each cross-section shall satisfy:

$$\frac{M_{Ed}}{M_{c,Rd}} \leq 1,0$$

where $M_{c,Rd}$ is determined considering fastener holes, see (4) to (6).

(2) The design resistance for bending about one principal axis of a cross-section is determined as follows:

$$M_{c,Rd} = M_{pl,Rd} = \frac{W_{pl} fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

for class 1 or 2 cross sections

$$M_{c,Rd} = M_{el,Rd} = \frac{W_{el,\min} fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

for class 3 cross sections

$$M_{c,Rd} = \frac{W_{eff,\min} fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

for class 4 cross sections

where $W_{el,\min}$ and $W_{eff,\min}$ corresponds to the fibre with the maximum elastic stress.

(3) For bending about both axes, the methods given in 6.2.9 should be used.

(4) Fastener holes in the tension flange may be ignored provided that for the tension flange:

$$\frac{A_{f,net} 0,9 fu}{\gamma_{M2}} \geq \frac{A_f fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

where $A_f$ is the area of the tension flange.

NOTE The criterion in (4) provides capacity design (see 1.5.8).

(5) Fastener holes in tension zone of the web need not be allowed for, provided that the limit given in (4) is satisfied for the complete tension zone comprising the tension flange plus the tension zone of the web.

(6) Fastener holes except for oversize and slotted holes in compression zone of the cross-section need not be allowed for, provided that they are …

4. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 6.2.4(1) — Compression resistance

(1)P The design value of the compression force $N_{Ed}$ at each cross-section shall satisfy:

$$\frac{N_{Ed}}{N_{c,Rd}} \leq 1,0$$

(2) The design resistance of the cross-section for uniform compression $N_{c,Rd}$ should be determined as follows:

$$N_{c,Rd} = \frac{A fy}{\gamma_{M0}} \text{ for class 1, 2 or 3 cross-sections}$$

$$N_{c,Rd} = \frac{A_{eff} fy}{\gamma_{M0}} \text{ for class 4 cross-sections}$$

(3) Fastener holes except for oversize and slotted holes as defined in EN 1090 need not be allowed for in compression members, provided that they are filled by fasteners.

(4) In the case of unsymmetrical Class 4 sections, the method given in 6.2.9.3 should be used to allow for the additional moment $\Delta M_{Ed}$ due to the eccentricity of the centroidal axis of the effective section, see 6.2.2.5(4).

5. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 6.2.6(1) — Shear resistance

(1)P The design value of the shear force $V_{Ed}$ at each cross section shall satisfy:

$$\frac{V_{Ed}}{V_{c,Rd}} \leq 1,0$$

where $V_{c,Rd}$ is the design shear resistance. For plastic design $V_{c,Rd}$ is the design plastic shear resistance $V_{pl,Rd}$ as given in (2). For elastic design $V_{c,Rd}$ is the design elastic shear resistance calculated using (4) and (5).

(2) In the absence of torsion the design plastic shear resistance is given by:

$$V_{pl,Rd} = \frac{A_v \left( fy / \sqrt{3} \right)}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

where $A_v$ is the shear area.

(3) The shear area $A_v$ may be taken as follows:

a) rolled I and H sections, load parallel to web
$$A - 2bt_f + (t_w + 2r)t_f$$ but not less than $\eta h_w t_w$

b) rolled channel sections, load parallel to web
$$A - 2bt_f + (t_w + r)t_f$$

c) rolled T-section, load parallel to web

- for rolled T-sections:
$$A_v = A - bt_f + (t_w + 2r)\frac{t_f}{2}$$

- for welded T-sections:
$$A_v = t_w(h - \frac{t_f}{2})$$

d) welded I, H and box sections, load parallel to web
$$\eta \sum (h_w t_w)$$

e) welded I, H, channel and box sections, load parallel to flanges
$$A - \sum (h_w t_w)$$

f) rolled rectangular hollow sections of uniform thickness:

- load parallel to depth
Ah/(b+h)

- load parallel to width
Ab/(b+h)

g) circular hollow sections and tubes of uniform thickness
$$2A/\pi$$

where $A$ is the crosssectional area;

--- **References:**
1. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 5.5.2(1) — Classification of cross-sections

(1) Four classes of cross-sections are defined, as follows:

- Class 1 cross-sections are those which can form a plastic hinge with the rotation capacity required from plastic analysis without reduction of the resistance.

- Class 2 cross-sections are those which can develop their plastic moment resistance, but have limited rotation capacity because of local buckling.

- Class 3 cross-sections are those in which the stress in the extreme compression fibre of the steel member assuming an elastic distribution of stresses can reach the yield strength, but local buckling is liable to prevent development of the plastic moment resistance.

- Class 4 cross-sections are those in which local buckling will occur before the attainment of yield stress in one or more parts of the cross-section.

(2) In Class 4 cross sections effective widths may be used to make the necessary allowances for reductions in resistance due to the effects of local buckling, see EN 1993-1-5, 4.4 AC2.

(3) The classification of a cross-section depends on the width to thickness ratio of the parts subject to compression.

(4) Compression parts include every part of a cross-section which is either totally or partially in compression under the load combination considered.

(5) The various compression parts in a cross-section (such as a web or flange) can, in general, be in different classes.

(6) A cross-section is cla …

2. EN 1993-1-1, Table 5.2 — Width-to-thickness limits

Angles

Refer also to “Outstand flanges” (see sheet 2 of 3)

Does not apply to angles in continuous contact with other components

Class

Section in compression

Stress distribution across section (compression positive)

$$h/t \leq 15\varepsilon$$ and $$\frac{b+h}{2t} \leq 11,5\varepsilon$$

Tubular sections

Class

Section in bending and/or compression

$$d/t \leq 50\varepsilon^2$$

$$d/t \leq 70\varepsilon^2$$

$$d/t \leq 90\varepsilon^2$$

NOTE For $d/t > 90\varepsilon^2$ see EN 1993-1-6.

$$\varepsilon = \sqrt{235/fy}$$

$$fy$$ 235 275 355 420 460

$$\varepsilon$$ 1,00 0,92 0,81 0,75 0,71

$$\varepsilon^2$$ 1,00 0,85 0,66 0,56 0,51

3. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 6.2.5(1) — Bending resistance

(1)P The design value of the bending moment $M_{Ed}$ at each cross-section shall satisfy:

$$\frac{M_{Ed}}{M_{c,Rd}} \leq 1,0$$

where $M_{c,Rd}$ is determined considering fastener holes, see (4) to (6).

(2) The design resistance for bending about one principal axis of a cross-section is determined as follows:

$$M_{c,Rd} = M_{pl,Rd} = \frac{W_{pl} fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

for class 1 or 2 cross sections

$$M_{c,Rd} = M_{el,Rd} = \frac{W_{el,\min} fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

for class 3 cross sections

$$M_{c,Rd} = \frac{W_{eff,\min} fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

for class 4 cross sections

where $W_{el,\min}$ and $W_{eff,\min}$ corresponds to the fibre with the maximum elastic stress.

(3) For bending about both axes, the methods given in 6.2.9 should be used.

(4) Fastener holes in the tension flange may be ignored provided that for the tension flange:

$$\frac{A_{f,net} 0,9 fu}{\gamma_{M2}} \geq \frac{A_f fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

where $A_f$ is the area of the tension flange.

NOTE The criterion in (4) provides capacity design (see 1.5.8).

(5) Fastener holes in tension zone of the web need not be allowed for, provided that the limit given in (4) is satisfied for the complete tension zone comprising the tension flange plus the tension zone of the web.

(6) Fastener holes except for oversize and slotted holes in compression zone of the cross-section need not be allowed for, provided that they are …

4. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 6.2.4(1) — Compression resistance

(1)P The design value of the compression force $N_{Ed}$ at each cross-section shall satisfy:

$$\frac{N_{Ed}}{N_{c,Rd}} \leq 1,0$$

(2) The design resistance of the cross-section for uniform compression $N_{c,Rd}$ should be determined as follows:

$$N_{c,Rd} = \frac{A fy}{\gamma_{M0}} \text{ for class 1, 2 or 3 cross-sections}$$

$$N_{c,Rd} = \frac{A_{eff} fy}{\gamma_{M0}} \text{ for class 4 cross-sections}$$

(3) Fastener holes except for oversize and slotted holes as defined in EN 1090 need not be allowed for in compression members, provided that they are filled by fasteners.

(4) In the case of unsymmetrical Class 4 sections, the method given in 6.2.9.3 should be used to allow for the additional moment $\Delta M_{Ed}$ due to the eccentricity of the centroidal axis of the effective section, see 6.2.2.5(4).

5. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 6.2.6(1) — Shear resistance

(1)P The design value of the shear force $V_{Ed}$ at each cross section shall satisfy:

$$\frac{V_{Ed}}{V_{c,Rd}} \leq 1,0$$

where $V_{c,Rd}$ is the design shear resistance. For plastic design $V_{c,Rd}$ is the design plastic shear resistance $V_{pl,Rd}$ as given in (2). For elastic design $V_{c,Rd}$ is the design elastic shear resistance calculated using (4) and (5).

(2) In the absence of torsion the design plastic shear resistance is given by:

$$V_{pl,Rd} = \frac{A_v \left( fy / \sqrt{3} \right)}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

where $A_v$ is the shear area.

(3) The shear area $A_v$ may be taken as follows:

a) rolled I and H sections, load parallel to web
$$A - 2bt_f + (t_w + 2r)t_f$$ but not less than $\eta h_w t_w$

b) rolled channel sections, load parallel to web
$$A - 2bt_f + (t_w + r)t_f$$

c) rolled T-section, load parallel to web

- for rolled T-sections:
$$A_v = A - bt_f + (t_w + 2r)\frac{t_f}{2}$$

- for welded T-sections:
$$A_v = t_w(h - \frac{t_f}{2})$$

d) welded I, H and box sections, load parallel to web
$$\eta \sum (h_w t_w)$$

e) welded I, H, channel and box sections, load parallel to flanges
$$A - \sum (h_w t_w)$$

f) rolled rectangular hollow sections of uniform thickness:

- load parallel to depth
Ah/(b+h)

- load parallel to width
Ab/(b+h)

g) circular hollow sections and tubes of uniform thickness
$$2A/\pi$$

where $A$ is the crosssectional area;

1. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 5.5.2(1) — Classification of cross-sections

(1) Four classes of cross-sections are defined, as follows:

- Class 1 cross-sections are those which can form a plastic hinge with the rotation capacity required from plastic analysis without reduction of the resistance.

- Class 2 cross-sections are those which can develop their plastic moment resistance, but have limited rotation capacity because of local buckling.

- Class 3 cross-sections are those in which the stress in the extreme compression fibre of the steel member assuming an elastic distribution of stresses can reach the yield strength, but local buckling is liable to prevent development of the plastic moment resistance.

- Class 4 cross-sections are those in which local buckling will occur before the attainment of yield stress in one or more parts of the cross-section.

(2) In Class 4 cross sections effective widths may be used to make the necessary allowances for reductions in resistance due to the effects of local buckling, see EN 1993-1-5, 4.4 AC2.

(3) The classification of a cross-section depends on the width to thickness ratio of the parts subject to compression.

(4) Compression parts include every part of a cross-section which is either totally or partially in compression under the load combination considered.

(5) The various compression parts in a cross-section (such as a web or flange) can, in general, be in different classes.

(6) A cross-section is cla …

Does not apply to angles in continuous contact with other components

3. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 6.2.5(1) — Bending resistance

(1)P The design value of the bending moment $M_{Ed}$ at each cross-section shall satisfy:

$$\frac{M_{Ed}}{M_{c,Rd}} \leq 1,0$$

where $M_{c,Rd}$ is determined considering fastener holes, see (4) to (6).

(2) The design resistance for bending about one principal axis of a cross-section is determined as follows:

$$M_{c,Rd} = M_{pl,Rd} = \frac{W_{pl} fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

for class 1 or 2 cross sections

$$M_{c,Rd} = M_{el,Rd} = \frac{W_{el,\min} fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

for class 3 cross sections

$$M_{c,Rd} = \frac{W_{eff,\min} fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

for class 4 cross sections

where $W_{el,\min}$ and $W_{eff,\min}$ corresponds to the fibre with the maximum elastic stress.

(3) For bending about both axes, the methods given in 6.2.9 should be used.

(4) Fastener holes in the tension flange may be ignored provided that for the tension flange:

$$\frac{A_{f,net} 0,9 fu}{\gamma_{M2}} \geq \frac{A_f fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

where $A_f$ is the area of the tension flange.

NOTE The criterion in (4) provides capacity design (see 1.5.8).

(5) Fastener holes in tension zone of the web need not be allowed for, provided that the limit given in (4) is satisfied for the complete tension zone comprising the tension flange plus the tension zone of the web.

(6) Fastener holes except for oversize and slotted holes in compression zone of the cross-section need not be allowed for, provided that they are …

4. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 6.2.4(1) — Compression resistance

(1)P The design value of the compression force $N_{Ed}$ at each cross-section shall satisfy:

$$\frac{N_{Ed}}{N_{c,Rd}} \leq 1,0$$

(2) The design resistance of the cross-section for uniform compression $N_{c,Rd}$ should be determined as follows:

$$N_{c,Rd} = \frac{A fy}{\gamma_{M0}} \text{ for class 1, 2 or 3 cross-sections}$$

$$N_{c,Rd} = \frac{A_{eff} fy}{\gamma_{M0}} \text{ for class 4 cross-sections}$$

(3) Fastener holes except for oversize and slotted holes as defined in EN 1090 need not be allowed for in compression members, provided that they are filled by fasteners.

(4) In the case of unsymmetrical Class 4 sections, the method given in 6.2.9.3 should be used to allow for the additional moment $\Delta M_{Ed}$ due to the eccentricity of the centroidal axis of the effective section, see 6.2.2.5(4).

5. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 6.2.6(1) — Shear resistance

(1)P The design value of the shear force $V_{Ed}$ at each cross section shall satisfy:

$$\frac{V_{Ed}}{V_{c,Rd}} \leq 1,0$$

where $V_{c,Rd}$ is the design shear resistance. For plastic design $V_{c,Rd}$ is the design plastic shear resistance $V_{pl,Rd}$ as given in (2). For elastic design $V_{c,Rd}$ is the design elastic shear resistance calculated using (4) and (5).

(2) In the absence of torsion the design plastic shear resistance is given by:

$$V_{pl,Rd} = \frac{A_v \left( fy / \sqrt{3} \right)}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

where $A_v$ is the shear area.

(3) The shear area $A_v$ may be taken as follows:

a) rolled I and H sections, load parallel to web
$$A - 2bt_f + (t_w + 2r)t_f$$ but not less than $\eta h_w t_w$

b) rolled channel sections, load parallel to web
$$A - 2bt_f + (t_w + r)t_f$$

c) rolled T-section, load parallel to web

- for rolled T-sections:
$$A_v = A - bt_f + (t_w + 2r)\frac{t_f}{2}$$

- for welded T-sections:
$$A_v = t_w(h - \frac{t_f}{2})$$

d) welded I, H and box sections, load parallel to web
$$\eta \sum (h_w t_w)$$

e) welded I, H, channel and box sections, load parallel to flanges
$$A - \sum (h_w t_w)$$

f) rolled rectangular hollow sections of uniform thickness:

- load parallel to depth
Ah/(b+h)

- load parallel to width
Ab/(b+h)

g) circular hollow sections and tubes of uniform thickness
$$2A/\pi$$

response.answer:
This follows EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 1.1.1 (Scope of Eurocode) with supporting classification guidance from Cl. 1.1.2 (Scope of Part 1.1 of Eurocode). --- **References:**
1. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 1.1.1 — Scope of Eurocode

(1) Eurocode 3 applies to the design of buildings and civil engineering works in steel. It complies with the principles and requirements for the safety and serviceability of structures, the basis of their design and verification that are given in EN 1990 – Basis of structural design.

(2) Eurocode 3 is concerned only with requirements for resistance, serviceability, durability and fire resistance of steel structures. Other requirements, e.g. concerning thermal or sound insulation, are not covered.

(3) Eurocode 3 is intended to be used in conjunction with:

- EN 1990 “Basis of structural design”
- EN 1991 “Actions on structures”
- ENs, ETAGs and ETAs for construction products relevant for steel structures
- EN 1090 “Execution of Steel Structures – Technical requirements”
- EN 1992 to EN 1999 when steel structures or steel components are referred to

(4) Eurocode 3 is subdivided in various parts:

EN 1993-1 Design of Steel Structures: General rules and rules for buildings.
EN 1993-2 Design of Steel Structures: Steel bridges.
EN 1993-3 Design of Steel Structures: Towers, masts and chimneys.
EN 1993-4 Design of Steel Structures: Silos, tanks and pipelines.
EN 1993-5 Design of Steel Structures: Piling.
EN 1993-6 Design of Steel Structures: Crane supporting structures.

(5) EN 1993-2 to EN 1993-6 refer to the generic rules in EN 1993-1. The rules in parts EN 1993-2 to EN 1993-6 supp …

2. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 1.1.2 — Scope of Part 1.1 of Eurocode

(1) EN 1993-1-1 gives basic design rules for steel structures with material thicknesses $t \geq 3$ mm. It also gives supplementary provisions for the structural design of steel buildings. These supplementary provisions are indicated by the letter “B” after the paragraph number, thus $(\quad)$B.

NOTE For cold formed members and sheeting, see EN 1993-1-3 $(\quad)$B.

(2) The following subjects are dealt with in EN 1993-1-1:

Section 1: General
Section 2: Basis of design
Section 3: Materials
Section 4: Durability
Section 5: Structural analysis
Section 6: Ultimate limit states
Section 7: Serviceability limit states

(3) Sections 1 to 2 provide additional clauses to those given in EN 1990 “Basis of structural design”.

(4) Section 3 deals with material properties of products made of low alloy structural steels.

(5) Section 4 gives general rules for durability.

(6) Section 5 refers to the structural analysis of structures, in which the members can be modelled with sufficient accuracy as line elements for global analysis.

(7) Section 6 gives detailed rules for the design of cross sections and members.

(8) Section 7 gives rules for serviceability.

response.answer:
This follows EN 1993-1-3, Cl. 10.1.5.1 (Lateral spring stiffness) with supporting guidance from EN 1993-1-11, Cl. A.4.3 (Strands and complete cables). --- **References:**
1. EN 1993-1-3, Cl. 10.1.5.1 — Lateral spring stiffness

(1) The lateral spring support given to the free flange of the purlin by the sheeting should be modelled as a lateral spring acting at the free flange, see figure 10.1. The total lateral spring stiffness $K$ per unit length should be determined from:

$$\frac{1}{K} = \frac{1}{K_A} + \frac{1}{K_B} + \frac{1}{K_C}$$

(10.11)

where:

- $K_A$ is the lateral stiffness corresponding to the rotational stiffness of the joint between the sheeting and the purlin;
- $K_B$ is the lateral stiffness due to distortion of the cross-section of the purlin;
- $K_C$ is the lateral stiffness due to the flexural stiffness of the sheeting.

(2) Normally it may be assumed to be safe as well as acceptable to neglect $1/K_C$ because $K_C$ is very large compared to $K_A$ and $K_B$. The value of $K$ should then be obtained from:

$$K = \frac{1}{\left( 1/K_A + 1/K_B \right)}$$

(10.12)

(3) The value of $(1/K_A + 1/K_B)$ may be obtained either by testing or by calculation.

NOTE: Appropriate testing procedures are given in Annex A.

(4) The lateral spring stiffness $K$ per unit length may be determined by calculation using:

$$\frac{1}{K} = \frac{4(1 - \nu^2)h^2(h_d + b_{\text{mod}})}{Et^3} + \frac{h^2}{C_D}$$

... (10.13)

in which the dimension $b_{\text{mod}}$ is determined as follows:

- for cases where the equivalent lateral force $q_{h,Ed}$ bringing the purlin into contact with the sheeting at the p …

response.answer:
Based on the image provided, here is a structural engineering interpretation of what is shown:

The image displays a classic static analysis model of a **simply supported beam** along with its corresponding internal force diagrams. 

Here is a breakdown of the specific elements:

*   **Structural System:** The top horizontal line represents a beam of length **$l$**. It is supported by a pinned support on the left (indicated by the fixed triangle) and a roller support on the right (indicated by the triangle with a line beneath it). The upward reaction forces at these supports are labeled **$R_a$** and **$R_b$**.
*   **Loading:** The yellow arrows pointing downward represent a **Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL)**, denoted by **$q$**, applied across the entire span of the beam.
*   **Bending Moment Diagram (M):** The middle section (red) shows the bending moment along the beam. Because the load is uniformly distributed, the bending moment forms a parabolic curve. The maximum bending moment, labeled **$M_{max}$**, occurs exactly at the mid-span of the beam. *(Note: The diagram is drawn on the tension side of the beam, which is standard practice in many European countries).*
*   **Shear Force Diagram (V):** The bottom section (red and blue) shows the shear force along the beam. It starts at a maximum positive value at the left support (**$V_a$**), decreases linearly to zero at the mid-span (exactly where the bending moment is at its maximum), and reaches a maximum negative value at the right support (**$V_b$**).

**Engineering Context:**
In the context of Eurocode 3 (EN 1993-1-1) for steel design, determining these maximum values ($M_{max}$ and $V_{a,b}$) is the first step required to perform cross-section resistance checks for bending and shear, as well as to check for lateral torsional buckling.

If you would like to perform a specific Eurocode 3 design check for a steel beam under these conditions, please provide the span length ($l$), the load magnitude ($q$), and your preferred steel grade or section size, and I can run the full calculation pipeline for you!

request.message: Given IPE300, S355, what is the bending resistance? Assume typical parameters if missing.

meta.rationale: Pure calculation intent detected; retrieval skipped in thinking mode.

assumptions: Assumed gamma_M0 = 1.0 as the standard partial safety factor for cross-section resistance.
Assumed section geometry and properties will be retrieved from the built-in library using the section_name.

response.answer:
For **IPE300** in **S355** steel, the design bending resistance is **MRd = 197.74 kNm**. This capacity is calculated using the formula **MRd = W * fy / γM0**, based on an applied section modulus of **W = 557.0 cm³**, a yield strength of **fy = 355.0 MPa**, and a partial factor of **γM0 = 1.0**. The profile is classified as a Class 4 section overall due to the slenderness of its web. This follows EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 5.5.2(1) (Classification of cross-sections) with supporting classification guidance from Cl. Table 5.2 (Width-to-thickness limits).

--- **References:**
1. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 5.5.2(1) — Classification of cross-sections

(1) Four classes of cross-sections are defined, as follows:

- Class 1 cross-sections are those which can form a plastic hinge with the rotation capacity required from plastic analysis without reduction of the resistance.

- Class 2 cross-sections are those which can develop their plastic moment resistance, but have limited rotation capacity because of local buckling.

- Class 3 cross-sections are those in which the stress in the extreme compression fibre of the steel member assuming an elastic distribution of stresses can reach the yield strength, but local buckling is liable to prevent development of the plastic moment resistance.

- Class 4 cross-sections are those in which local buckling will occur before the attainment of yield stress in one or more parts of the cross-section.

(2) In Class 4 cross sections effective widths may be used to make the necessary allowances for reductions in resistance due to the effects of local buckling, see EN 1993-1-5, 4.4 AC2.

(3) The classification of a cross-section depends on the width to thickness ratio of the parts subject to compression.

(4) Compression parts include every part of a cross-section which is either totally or partially in compression under the load combination considered.

(5) The various compression parts in a cross-section (such as a web or flange) can, in general, be in different classes.

(6) A cross-section is cla …

2. EN 1993-1-1, Table 5.2 — Width-to-thickness limits

Angles

Refer also to “Outstand flanges” (see sheet 2 of 3)

Does not apply to angles in continuous contact with other components

Class

Stress distribution across section (compression positive)

$$h/t \leq 15\varepsilon$$ and $$\frac{b+h}{2t} \leq 11,5\varepsilon$$

Tubular sections

$$d/t \leq 50\varepsilon^2$$

$$d/t \leq 70\varepsilon^2$$

$$d/t \leq 90\varepsilon^2$$

NOTE For $d/t > 90\varepsilon^2$ see EN 1993-1-6.

$$\varepsilon = \sqrt{235/fy}$$

$$fy$$ 235 275 355 420 460

$$\varepsilon$$ 1,00 0,92 0,81 0,75 0,71

$$\varepsilon^2$$ 1,00 0,85 0,66 0,56 0,51

3. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 6.2.5(1) — Bending resistance

(1)P The design value of the bending moment $M_{Ed}$ at each cross-section shall satisfy:

$$\frac{M_{Ed}}{M_{c,Rd}} \leq 1,0$$

where $M_{c,Rd}$ is determined considering fastener holes, see (4) to (6).

(2) The design resistance for bending about one principal axis of a cross-section is determined as follows:

$$M_{c,Rd} = M_{pl,Rd} = \frac{W_{pl} fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

for class 1 or 2 cross sections

$$M_{c,Rd} = M_{el,Rd} = \frac{W_{el,\min} fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

for class 3 cross sections

$$M_{c,Rd} = \frac{W_{eff,\min} fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

for class 4 cross sections

where $W_{el,\min}$ and $W_{eff,\min}$ corresponds to the fibre with the maximum elastic stress.

(3) For bending about both axes, the methods given in 6.2.9 should be used.

(4) Fastener holes in the tension flange may be ignored provided that for the tension flange:

$$\frac{A_{f,net} 0,9 fu}{\gamma_{M2}} \geq \frac{A_f fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

where $A_f$ is the area of the tension flange.

NOTE The criterion in (4) provides capacity design (see 1.5.8).

(5) Fastener holes in tension zone of the web need not be allowed for, provided that the limit given in (4) is satisfied for the complete tension zone comprising the tension flange plus the tension zone of the web.

(6) Fastener holes except for oversize and slotted holes in compression zone of the cross-section need not be allowed for, provided that they are …

4. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 6.2.4(1) — Compression resistance

(1)P The design value of the compression force $N_{Ed}$ at each cross-section shall satisfy:

$$\frac{N_{Ed}}{N_{c,Rd}} \leq 1,0$$

(2) The design resistance of the cross-section for uniform compression $N_{c,Rd}$ should be determined as follows:

$$N_{c,Rd} = \frac{A fy}{\gamma_{M0}} \text{ for class 1, 2 or 3 cross-sections}$$

$$N_{c,Rd} = \frac{A_{eff} fy}{\gamma_{M0}} \text{ for class 4 cross-sections}$$

(3) Fastener holes except for oversize and slotted holes as defined in EN 1090 need not be allowed for in compression members, provided that they are filled by fasteners.

(4) In the case of unsymmetrical Class 4 sections, the method given in 6.2.9.3 should be used to allow for the additional moment $\Delta M_{Ed}$ due to the eccentricity of the centroidal axis of the effective section, see 6.2.2.5(4).

5. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 6.2.6(1) — Shear resistance

(1)P The design value of the shear force $V_{Ed}$ at each cross section shall satisfy:

$$\frac{V_{Ed}}{V_{c,Rd}} \leq 1,0$$

where $V_{c,Rd}$ is the design shear resistance. For plastic design $V_{c,Rd}$ is the design plastic shear resistance $V_{pl,Rd}$ as given in (2). For elastic design $V_{c,Rd}$ is the design elastic shear resistance calculated using (4) and (5).

(2) In the absence of torsion the design plastic shear resistance is given by:

$$V_{pl,Rd} = \frac{A_v \left( fy / \sqrt{3} \right)}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

where $A_v$ is the shear area.

(3) The shear area $A_v$ may be taken as follows:

a) rolled I and H sections, load parallel to web
$$A - 2bt_f + (t_w + 2r)t_f$$ but not less than $\eta h_w t_w$

b) rolled channel sections, load parallel to web
$$A - 2bt_f + (t_w + r)t_f$$

c) rolled T-section, load parallel to web

- for rolled T-sections:
$$A_v = A - bt_f + (t_w + 2r)\frac{t_f}{2}$$

- for welded T-sections:
$$A_v = t_w(h - \frac{t_f}{2})$$

d) welded I, H and box sections, load parallel to web
$$\eta \sum (h_w t_w)$$

e) welded I, H, channel and box sections, load parallel to flanges
$$A - \sum (h_w t_w)$$

f) rolled rectangular hollow sections of uniform thickness:

- load parallel to depth
Ah/(b+h)

- load parallel to width
Ab/(b+h)

g) circular hollow sections and tubes of uniform thickness
$$2A/\pi$$

where $A$ is the crosssectional area;

meta.rationale: Query requires both code evidence and numeric calculation. | Kept initial tool plan after retrieval.

assumptions: Assumed steel grade S355 as none was specified.
Assumed section IPE300 as none was specified.
Assumed partial safety factor gamma_M0 = 1.0.

response.answer:
For **IPE300** in **S355** steel, the design bending resistance is **MRd = 197.74 kNm**. This capacity is determined using the base formula **MRd = W * fy / γM0**, utilizing a section modulus of **W = 557.0 cm³** and a yield strength of **fy = 355.0 MPa** for the Class 4 profile. In accordance with clause 6.2.5(1)P, the design bending moment must satisfy the condition that the ratio of the applied moment to this resistance is less than or equal to 1.0. This follows EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 5.5.2(1) (Classification of cross-sections) with supporting classification guidance from Cl. Table 5.2 (Width-to-thickness limits).
Section Classification detailed results

shear formula basisVRd = A_v * fy / (sqrt(3) * γM0)
**Notes** - Member buckling and lateral torsional buckling are outside this MVP placeholder calculator.
Assumptions used - Assumed steel grade S355 as none was specified. - Assumed section IPE300 as none was specified. - Assumed partial safety factor γM0 = 1.0.
--- **References:**
1. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 5.5.2(1) — Classification of cross-sections

(1) Four classes of cross-sections are defined, as follows:

- Class 1 cross-sections are those which can form a plastic hinge with the rotation capacity required from plastic analysis without reduction of the resistance.

- Class 2 cross-sections are those which can develop their plastic moment resistance, but have limited rotation capacity because of local buckling.

- Class 3 cross-sections are those in which the stress in the extreme compression fibre of the steel member assuming an elastic distribution of stresses can reach the yield strength, but local buckling is liable to prevent development of the plastic moment resistance.

- Class 4 cross-sections are those in which local buckling will occur before the attainment of yield stress in one or more parts of the cross-section.

(2) In Class 4 cross sections effective widths may be used to make the necessary allowances for reductions in resistance due to the effects of local buckling, see EN 1993-1-5, 4.4 AC2.

(3) The classification of a cross-section depends on the width to thickness ratio of the parts subject to compression.

(4) Compression parts include every part of a cross-section which is either totally or partially in compression under the load combination considered.

(5) The various compression parts in a cross-section (such as a web or flange) can, in general, be in different classes.

(6) A cross-section is cla …

2. EN 1993-1-1, Table 5.2 — Width-to-thickness limits

Angles

Refer also to “Outstand flanges” (see sheet 2 of 3)

Does not apply to angles in continuous contact with other components

Class

Section in compression

Stress distribution across section (compression positive)

$$h/t \leq 15\varepsilon$$ and $$\frac{b+h}{2t} \leq 11,5\varepsilon$$

Tubular sections

Class

Section in bending and/or compression

$$d/t \leq 50\varepsilon^2$$

$$d/t \leq 70\varepsilon^2$$

$$d/t \leq 90\varepsilon^2$$

NOTE For $d/t > 90\varepsilon^2$ see EN 1993-1-6.

$$\varepsilon = \sqrt{235/fy}$$

$$fy$$ 235 275 355 420 460

$$\varepsilon$$ 1,00 0,92 0,81 0,75 0,71

$$\varepsilon^2$$ 1,00 0,85 0,66 0,56 0,51

3. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 6.2.5(1) — Bending resistance

(1)P The design value of the bending moment $M_{Ed}$ at each cross-section shall satisfy:

$$\frac{M_{Ed}}{M_{c,Rd}} \leq 1,0$$

where $M_{c,Rd}$ is determined considering fastener holes, see (4) to (6).

(2) The design resistance for bending about one principal axis of a cross-section is determined as follows:

$$M_{c,Rd} = M_{pl,Rd} = \frac{W_{pl} fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

for class 1 or 2 cross sections

$$M_{c,Rd} = M_{el,Rd} = \frac{W_{el,\min} fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

for class 3 cross sections

$$M_{c,Rd} = \frac{W_{eff,\min} fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

for class 4 cross sections

where $W_{el,\min}$ and $W_{eff,\min}$ corresponds to the fibre with the maximum elastic stress.

(3) For bending about both axes, the methods given in 6.2.9 should be used.

(4) Fastener holes in the tension flange may be ignored provided that for the tension flange:

$$\frac{A_{f,net} 0,9 fu}{\gamma_{M2}} \geq \frac{A_f fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

where $A_f$ is the area of the tension flange.

NOTE The criterion in (4) provides capacity design (see 1.5.8).

(5) Fastener holes in tension zone of the web need not be allowed for, provided that the limit given in (4) is satisfied for the complete tension zone comprising the tension flange plus the tension zone of the web.

(6) Fastener holes except for oversize and slotted holes in compression zone of the cross-section need not be allowed for, provided that they are …

4. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 6.2.4(1) — Compression resistance

(1)P The design value of the compression force $N_{Ed}$ at each cross-section shall satisfy:

$$\frac{N_{Ed}}{N_{c,Rd}} \leq 1,0$$

(2) The design resistance of the cross-section for uniform compression $N_{c,Rd}$ should be determined as follows:

$$N_{c,Rd} = \frac{A fy}{\gamma_{M0}} \text{ for class 1, 2 or 3 cross-sections}$$

$$N_{c,Rd} = \frac{A_{eff} fy}{\gamma_{M0}} \text{ for class 4 cross-sections}$$

(3) Fastener holes except for oversize and slotted holes as defined in EN 1090 need not be allowed for in compression members, provided that they are filled by fasteners.

(4) In the case of unsymmetrical Class 4 sections, the method given in 6.2.9.3 should be used to allow for the additional moment $\Delta M_{Ed}$ due to the eccentricity of the centroidal axis of the effective section, see 6.2.2.5(4).

5. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 6.2.6(1) — Shear resistance

(1)P The design value of the shear force $V_{Ed}$ at each cross section shall satisfy:

$$\frac{V_{Ed}}{V_{c,Rd}} \leq 1,0$$

where $V_{c,Rd}$ is the design shear resistance. For plastic design $V_{c,Rd}$ is the design plastic shear resistance $V_{pl,Rd}$ as given in (2). For elastic design $V_{c,Rd}$ is the design elastic shear resistance calculated using (4) and (5).

(2) In the absence of torsion the design plastic shear resistance is given by:

$$V_{pl,Rd} = \frac{A_v \left( fy / \sqrt{3} \right)}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

where $A_v$ is the shear area.

(3) The shear area $A_v$ may be taken as follows:

a) rolled I and H sections, load parallel to web
$$A - 2bt_f + (t_w + 2r)t_f$$ but not less than $\eta h_w t_w$

b) rolled channel sections, load parallel to web
$$A - 2bt_f + (t_w + r)t_f$$

c) rolled T-section, load parallel to web

- for rolled T-sections:
$$A_v = A - bt_f + (t_w + 2r)\frac{t_f}{2}$$

- for welded T-sections:
$$A_v = t_w(h - \frac{t_f}{2})$$

d) welded I, H and box sections, load parallel to web
$$\eta \sum (h_w t_w)$$

e) welded I, H, channel and box sections, load parallel to flanges
$$A - \sum (h_w t_w)$$

f) rolled rectangular hollow sections of uniform thickness:

- load parallel to depth
Ah/(b+h)

- load parallel to width
Ab/(b+h)

g) circular hollow sections and tubes of uniform thickness
$$2A/\pi$$

where $A$ is the crosssectional area;

assumptions: Assumed steel grade S355 as none was specified.
Assumed section IPE300 as none was specified.
Assumed partial safety factor gamma_M0 = 1.0.

request.message: IPE300 S355 bending resistance

meta.rationale: Pure calculation intent detected; retrieval skipped in thinking mode.

assumptions: Assumed partial safety factor gamma_M0 = 1.0 for cross-section resistance as per standard Eurocode 3 recommendations.

response.answer:
For **IPE300** in **S355** steel, the design bending resistance is **MRd = 197.74 kNm**. This capacity is calculated using the formula **MRd = W * fy / γM0**, based on a section modulus of **W = 557.0 cm³**, a yield strength of **fy = 355.0 MPa**, and a partial safety factor of **γM0 = 1.0**. The profile is evaluated as a Class 4 section overall because the web falls into Class 4, despite the flanges being Class 1. This follows EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 5.5.2(1) (Classification of cross-sections) with supporting classification guidance from Cl. Table 5.2 (Width-to-thickness limits).
Section Classification detailed results

section nameIPE300
h (mm)300 mm
b (mm)150 mm
tw (mm)7.10 mm
tf (mm)10.70 mm
fy mpa355 MPa
OutputValue
web class4
flange class1
governing class4
**Notes** - This MVP uses simplified threshold values aligned to EC3 structure for demonstrative chaining.
Member Resistance detailed results
ParameterValue

steel gradeS355
fy mpa355 MPa
section class4
γM01
w used (cm³)557 cm³
area (cm²)53.80 cm²
av z (cm²)27.50 cm²
OutputValue
MRd (kNm)197.74 kNm
NRd (kN)1909.90 kN
VRd (kN)563.64 kN
bending formula basisMRd = W * fy / γM0
axial formula basisNRd = A * fy / γM0
shear formula basisVRd = A_v * fy / (sqrt(3) * γM0)
**Notes** - Member buckling and lateral torsional buckling are outside this MVP placeholder calculator.
Assumptions used - Assumed partial safety factor γM0 = 1.0 for cross-section resistance as per standard Eurocode 3 recommendations.
--- **References:**
1. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 5.5.2(1) — Classification of cross-sections

(1) Four classes of cross-sections are defined, as follows:

- Class 1 cross-sections are those which can form a plastic hinge with the rotation capacity required from plastic analysis without reduction of the resistance.

- Class 2 cross-sections are those which can develop their plastic moment resistance, but have limited rotation capacity because of local buckling.

- Class 3 cross-sections are those in which the stress in the extreme compression fibre of the steel member assuming an elastic distribution of stresses can reach the yield strength, but local buckling is liable to prevent development of the plastic moment resistance.

- Class 4 cross-sections are those in which local buckling will occur before the attainment of yield stress in one or more parts of the cross-section.

(2) In Class 4 cross sections effective widths may be used to make the necessary allowances for reductions in resistance due to the effects of local buckling, see EN 1993-1-5, 4.4 AC2.

(3) The classification of a cross-section depends on the width to thickness ratio of the parts subject to compression.

(4) Compression parts include every part of a cross-section which is either totally or partially in compression under the load combination considered.

(5) The various compression parts in a cross-section (such as a web or flange) can, in general, be in different classes.

(6) A cross-section is cla …

2. EN 1993-1-1, Table 5.2 — Width-to-thickness limits

Angles

Refer also to “Outstand flanges” (see sheet 2 of 3)

Does not apply to angles in continuous contact with other components

Class

Section in compression

Stress distribution across section (compression positive)

$$h/t \leq 15\varepsilon$$ and $$\frac{b+h}{2t} \leq 11,5\varepsilon$$

Tubular sections

Class

Section in bending and/or compression

$$d/t \leq 50\varepsilon^2$$

$$d/t \leq 70\varepsilon^2$$

$$d/t \leq 90\varepsilon^2$$

NOTE For $d/t > 90\varepsilon^2$ see EN 1993-1-6.

$$\varepsilon = \sqrt{235/fy}$$

$$fy$$ 235 275 355 420 460

$$\varepsilon$$ 1,00 0,92 0,81 0,75 0,71

$$\varepsilon^2$$ 1,00 0,85 0,66 0,56 0,51

3. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 6.2.5(1) — Bending resistance

(1)P The design value of the bending moment $M_{Ed}$ at each cross-section shall satisfy:

$$\frac{M_{Ed}}{M_{c,Rd}} \leq 1,0$$

where $M_{c,Rd}$ is determined considering fastener holes, see (4) to (6).

(2) The design resistance for bending about one principal axis of a cross-section is determined as follows:

$$M_{c,Rd} = M_{pl,Rd} = \frac{W_{pl} fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

for class 1 or 2 cross sections

$$M_{c,Rd} = M_{el,Rd} = \frac{W_{el,\min} fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

for class 3 cross sections

$$M_{c,Rd} = \frac{W_{eff,\min} fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

for class 4 cross sections

where $W_{el,\min}$ and $W_{eff,\min}$ corresponds to the fibre with the maximum elastic stress.

(3) For bending about both axes, the methods given in 6.2.9 should be used.

(4) Fastener holes in the tension flange may be ignored provided that for the tension flange:

$$\frac{A_{f,net} 0,9 fu}{\gamma_{M2}} \geq \frac{A_f fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

where $A_f$ is the area of the tension flange.

NOTE The criterion in (4) provides capacity design (see 1.5.8).

(5) Fastener holes in tension zone of the web need not be allowed for, provided that the limit given in (4) is satisfied for the complete tension zone comprising the tension flange plus the tension zone of the web.

(6) Fastener holes except for oversize and slotted holes in compression zone of the cross-section need not be allowed for, provided that they are …

4. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 6.2.4(1) — Compression resistance

(1)P The design value of the compression force $N_{Ed}$ at each cross-section shall satisfy:

$$\frac{N_{Ed}}{N_{c,Rd}} \leq 1,0$$

(2) The design resistance of the cross-section for uniform compression $N_{c,Rd}$ should be determined as follows:

$$N_{c,Rd} = \frac{A fy}{\gamma_{M0}} \text{ for class 1, 2 or 3 cross-sections}$$

$$N_{c,Rd} = \frac{A_{eff} fy}{\gamma_{M0}} \text{ for class 4 cross-sections}$$

(3) Fastener holes except for oversize and slotted holes as defined in EN 1090 need not be allowed for in compression members, provided that they are filled by fasteners.

(4) In the case of unsymmetrical Class 4 sections, the method given in 6.2.9.3 should be used to allow for the additional moment $\Delta M_{Ed}$ due to the eccentricity of the centroidal axis of the effective section, see 6.2.2.5(4).

5. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 6.2.6(1) — Shear resistance

(1)P The design value of the shear force $V_{Ed}$ at each cross section shall satisfy:

$$\frac{V_{Ed}}{V_{c,Rd}} \leq 1,0$$

where $V_{c,Rd}$ is the design shear resistance. For plastic design $V_{c,Rd}$ is the design plastic shear resistance $V_{pl,Rd}$ as given in (2). For elastic design $V_{c,Rd}$ is the design elastic shear resistance calculated using (4) and (5).

(2) In the absence of torsion the design plastic shear resistance is given by:

$$V_{pl,Rd} = \frac{A_v \left( fy / \sqrt{3} \right)}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

where $A_v$ is the shear area.

(3) The shear area $A_v$ may be taken as follows:

a) rolled I and H sections, load parallel to web
$$A - 2bt_f + (t_w + 2r)t_f$$ but not less than $\eta h_w t_w$

b) rolled channel sections, load parallel to web
$$A - 2bt_f + (t_w + r)t_f$$

c) rolled T-section, load parallel to web

- for rolled T-sections:
$$A_v = A - bt_f + (t_w + 2r)\frac{t_f}{2}$$

- for welded T-sections:
$$A_v = t_w(h - \frac{t_f}{2})$$

d) welded I, H and box sections, load parallel to web
$$\eta \sum (h_w t_w)$$

e) welded I, H, channel and box sections, load parallel to flanges
$$A - \sum (h_w t_w)$$

f) rolled rectangular hollow sections of uniform thickness:

- load parallel to depth
Ah/(b+h)

- load parallel to width
Ab/(b+h)

g) circular hollow sections and tubes of uniform thickness
$$2A/\pi$$

where $A$ is the crosssectional area;

b is the …
assumptions: Assumed partial safety factor gamma_M0 = 1.0 for cross-section resistance as per standard Eurocode 3 recommendations.

notes: This MVP uses simplified threshold values aligned to EC3 structure for demonstrative chaining.
Member buckling and lateral torsional buckling are outside this MVP placeholder calculator.

top.title: Design resistance for individual fasteners subjected to shear and/or tension
Joints loaded in shear subject to impact, vibration and/or load reversal

Joints loaded in shear subject to impact, vibration and/or load reversal

machine_events.title: Input Resolution
machine_events.detail: No calculator inputs required for this path.

machine_events.title: MCP Tools

response.answer:
The design shear resistance per shear plane is defined as **Fv,Rd = αv * fub * A / γM2**. According to Table 3.4, this check verifies that an individual fastener can safely carry the applied shear load, taking the area **A** as the tensile stress area **A_s** if the shear plane passes through the threaded portion of the bolt. As outlined in clause 3.4.1, this calculation is used for Category A bearing-type connections utilizing bolt classes 4.6 up to 10.9 without the need for preloading. This follows EN 1993-1-8, Cl. Table 3.4 (Design resistance for individual fasteners subjected to shear and/or tension) with supporting classification guidance from Cl. 2.6 (Joints loaded in shear subject to impact, vibration and/or load reversal). --- **References:**
1. EN 1993-1-8, Cl. 2.6 — Joints loaded in shear subject to impact, vibration and/or load reversal

(1) Where a joint loaded in shear is subject to impact or significant vibration one of the following jointing methods should be used:

- welding
- bolts with locking devices
- preloaded bolts
- injection bolts
- other types of bolt which effectively prevent movement of the connected parts
- rivets.

(2) Where slip is not acceptable in a joint (because it is subject to reversal of shear load or for any other reason), preloaded bolts in a Category B or C connection (see 3.4), fit bolts (see 3.6.1), rivets or welding should be used.

(3) For wind and/or stability bracings, bolts in Category A connections (see 3.4) may be used.

response.answer:
A bolt shear check verifies the fastener's capacity against applied forces, primarily governed by the bearing resistance **F_b,Rd = 2.5 α_b k_t fu d t / γM2** and the net-section resistance **F_n,Rd**. According to Table 8.4, the bearing calculation depends on the ultimate strength, bolt diameter, plate thickness, and specific factors like **α_b** and **k_t**. Clause 3.6.1 states that the design resistance for an individual fastener subjected to shear must be evaluated using these tabulated limits to ensure structural adequacy. This follows EN 1993-1-3, Cl. Table 10.4 (Shear force and tensile force in fastener along the beam) with supporting classification guidance from Cl. 3.3.2 (Other types of mechanical fastener). --- **References:**
1. EN 1993-1-3, Cl. 3.3.2 — Other types of mechanical fastener

(1) Other types of mechanical fasteners as:

- self-tapping screws as thread forming self-tapping screws, thread cutting self-tapping screws or self-drilling self-tapping screws,
- cartridge-fired pins,
- blind rivets

may be used where they comply with the relevant European Product Specification.

(2) The characteristic shear resistance $F_{\text{v,Rk}}$ and the characteristic minimum tension resistance $F_{\text{t,Rk}}$ of the mechanical fasteners may be taken from the EN Product Standard or ETAG or ETA.

response.answer:
The design bending resistance is **M_c,Rd = 223.08 kNm**. According to EN 1993-1-1 clause 6.2.5, the design bending moment must satisfy the condition **MEd / M_c,Rd <= 1.0**. As per clause 6.2.8, this moment resistance requires no reduction for shear provided the coincident shear force is less than half the plastic shear resistance. This follows EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 6.2.10 (Bending, shear and axial force) with supporting classification guidance from Cl. 6.2.5 (Bending moment). --- **References:**
1. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 6.2.10 — Bending, shear and axial force

(1) Where shear and axial force are present, allowance should be made for the effect of both shear force and axial force on the resistance moment.

(2) Provided that the design value of the shear force $V_{Ed}$ does not exceed 50% of the design plastic shear resistance $V_{pl,Rd}$ no reduction of the resistances defined for bending and axial force in 6.2.9 need be made, except where shear buckling reduces the section resistance, see EN 1993-1-5.

(3) Where $V_{Ed}$ exceeds 50% of $V_{pl,Rd}$ the design resistance of the cross-section to combinations of moment and axial force should be calculated using a reduced yield strength

$$(1-\rho) fy$$

(6.45)

for the shear area

where $\rho = (2V_{Ed} / V_{pl,Rd} - 1)^2$ and $V_{pl,Rd}$ is obtained from 6.2.6(2).

NOTE Instead of reducing the yield strength also the plate thickness of the relevant part of the cross section may be reduced.

2. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 6.2.5 — Bending moment

(1)P The design value of the bending moment $M_{Ed}$ at each cross-section shall satisfy:

$$\frac{M_{Ed}}{M_{c,Rd}} \leq 1,0$$

where $M_{c,Rd}$ is determined considering fastener holes, see (4) to (6).

(2) The design resistance for bending about one principal axis of a cross-section is determined as follows:

$$M_{c,Rd} = M_{pl,Rd} = \frac{W_{pl} fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

for class 1 or 2 cross sections

$$M_{c,Rd} = M_{el,Rd} = \frac{W_{el,\min} fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

for class 3 cross sections

$$M_{c,Rd} = \frac{W_{eff,\min} fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

for class 4 cross sections

where $W_{el,\min}$ and $W_{eff,\min}$ corresponds to the fibre with the maximum elastic stress.

(3) For bending about both axes, the methods given in 6.2.9 should be used.

(4) Fastener holes in the tension flange may be ignored provided that for the tension flange:

$$\frac{A_{f,net} 0,9 fu}{\gamma_{M2}} \geq \frac{A_f fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

where $A_f$ is the area of the tension flange.

NOTE The criterion in (4) provides capacity design (see 1.5.8).

(5) Fastener holes in tension zone of the web need not be allowed for, provided that the limit given in (4) is satisfied for the complete tension zone comprising the tension flange plus the tension zone of the web.

response.answer:
The design bearing resistance for a bolt loaded in shear is evaluated as **F_b,Rd = 2.5 α_b k_t fu d t / γM2**. According to Table 8.4, this calculation determines the fastener's capacity based on the ultimate strength, bolt diameter, plate thickness, and a coefficient **α_b** taken as the smallest of 1.0 or the edge distance ratio. To complete the shear bolt check, the net-section resistance **F_n,Rd** must also be verified alongside the bearing capacity. This follows EN 1993-1-8, Cl. 6.4.2 (Bolted joints) with supporting classification guidance from Cl. 6.2.6.4.1 (Unstiffened column flange, bolted connection). --- **References:**
1. EN 1993-1-8, Cl. 6.4.2 — Bolted joints

(1) A beam-to-column joint in which the design moment resistance of the joint $M_{j,Rd}$ is governed by the design resistance of the column web panel in shear, may be assumed to have adequate rotation capacity for plastic global analysis, provided that $d_{wc}/t_w \leq 69\epsilon$.

(2) A joint with either a bolted end-plate or angle flange cleat connection may be assumed to have sufficient rotation capacity for plastic analysis, provided that both of the following conditions are satisfied:

a) the design moment resistance of the joint is governed by the design resistance of either:

- the column flange in bending or
- the beam end-plate or tension flange cleat in bending.

b) the thickness $t$ of either the column flange or the beam end-plate or tension flange cleat (not necessarily the same basic component as in (a)) satisfies:

$$t \leq 0.36d\sqrt{f_{ub}/fy}$$

... (6.32)

where:

$$fy$$ is the yield strength of the relevant basic component;

$$d$$ is the nominal diameter of the bolt;

$$f_{ub}$$ is the ultimate tensile strength of the bolt material.

(3) A joint with a bolted connection in which the design moment resistance $M_{j,Rd}$ is governed by the design resistance of its bolts in shear, should not be assumed to have sufficient rotation capacity for plastic global analysis.

2. EN 1993-1-8, Cl. 6.2.6.4.1 — Unstiffened column flange, bolted connection

(1) The design resistance and failure mode of an unstiffened column flange in tranverse bending, together with the associated bolts in tension, should be taken as similar to those of an equivalent T-stub flange, see 6.2.4, for both:

- each individual bolt-row required to resist tension;
- each group of bolt-rows required to resist tension.

(2) The dimensions $e_{min}$ and $m$ for use in 6.2.4 should be determined from Figure 6.8.

(3) The effective length of equivalent T-stub flange should be determined for the individual bolt-rows and the bolt-group in accordance with 6.2.4.2 from the values given for each bolt-row in Table 6.4.

BS EN 1993-1-8:2005
EN 1993-1-8:2005 (E)

a) Welded end-plate narrower than column flange.

b) Welded end-plate wider than column flange.

c) Angle flange cleats.

Figure 6.8: Definitions of $e$, $e_{\text{min}}$, $r_c$ and $m$

response.answer:
The design shear resistance per shear plane is evaluated as **Fv,Rd = (alpha_v * fub * A) / γM2**. Based on Table 3.4, the area **A** is taken as the tensile stress area **A_s** when the shear plane passes through the threaded portion of the bolt. As outlined in clause 3.4.1, this check is typically used for Category A bearing-type connections utilizing bolt classes 4.6 to 10.9 without the need for preloading. This follows EN 1993-1-8, Cl. Table 3.4 (Design resistance for individual fasteners subjected to shear and/or tension) with supporting classification guidance from Cl. 2.6 (Joints loaded in shear subject to impact, vibration and/or load reversal). --- **References:**
1. EN 1993-1-8, Cl. 2.6 — Joints loaded in shear subject to impact, vibration and/or load reversal

(1) Where a joint loaded in shear is subject to impact or significant vibration one of the following jointing methods should be used:

- welding
- bolts with locking devices
- preloaded bolts
- injection bolts
- other types of bolt which effectively prevent movement of the connected parts
- rivets.

(2) Where slip is not acceptable in a joint (because it is subject to reversal of shear load or for any other reason), preloaded bolts in a Category B or C connection (see 3.4), fit bolts (see 3.6.1), rivets or welding should be used.

(3) For wind and/or stability bracings, bolts in Category A connections (see 3.4) may be used.

response.answer:
The design bending resistance is **M_c,Rd = 223.08 kNm**. According to Clause 6.2.5, the cross-section is verified by ensuring the design bending moment does not exceed this resistance capacity. Additionally, per Clause 6.2.8, this full moment resistance can be utilized without reduction provided the coincident shear force remains below half of the plastic shear resistance. This follows EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 6.2.10 (Bending, shear and axial force) with supporting classification guidance from Cl. 6.2.5 (Bending moment). --- **References:**
1. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 6.2.10 — Bending, shear and axial force

(1) Where shear and axial force are present, allowance should be made for the effect of both shear force and axial force on the resistance moment.

(2) Provided that the design value of the shear force $V_{Ed}$ does not exceed 50% of the design plastic shear resistance $V_{pl,Rd}$ no reduction of the resistances defined for bending and axial force in 6.2.9 need be made, except where shear buckling reduces the section resistance, see EN 1993-1-5.

(3) Where $V_{Ed}$ exceeds 50% of $V_{pl,Rd}$ the design resistance of the cross-section to combinations of moment and axial force should be calculated using a reduced yield strength

$$(1-\rho) fy$$

(6.45)

for the shear area

where $\rho = (2V_{Ed} / V_{pl,Rd} - 1)^2$ and $V_{pl,Rd}$ is obtained from 6.2.6(2).

NOTE Instead of reducing the yield strength also the plate thickness of the relevant part of the cross section may be reduced.

2. EN 1993-1-1, Cl. 6.2.5 — Bending moment

(1)P The design value of the bending moment $M_{Ed}$ at each cross-section shall satisfy:

$$\frac{M_{Ed}}{M_{c,Rd}} \leq 1,0$$

where $M_{c,Rd}$ is determined considering fastener holes, see (4) to (6).

(2) The design resistance for bending about one principal axis of a cross-section is determined as follows:

$$M_{c,Rd} = M_{pl,Rd} = \frac{W_{pl} fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

for class 1 or 2 cross sections

$$M_{c,Rd} = M_{el,Rd} = \frac{W_{el,\min} fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

for class 3 cross sections

$$M_{c,Rd} = \frac{W_{eff,\min} fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

for class 4 cross sections

where $W_{el,\min}$ and $W_{eff,\min}$ corresponds to the fibre with the maximum elastic stress.

(3) For bending about both axes, the methods given in 6.2.9 should be used.

(4) Fastener holes in the tension flange may be ignored provided that for the tension flange:

$$\frac{A_{f,net} 0,9 fu}{\gamma_{M2}} \geq \frac{A_f fy}{\gamma_{M0}}$$

where $A_f$ is the area of the tension flange.

NOTE The criterion in (4) provides capacity design (see 1.5.8).

(5) Fastener holes in tension zone of the web need not be allowed for, provided that the limit given in (4) is satisfied for the complete tension zone comprising the tension flange plus the tension zone of the web.

(6) Fastener holes except for oversize and slotted holes in compression zone of the cross-section need not be allowed for, provided that they are …

response.answer:
The shear bolt check determines the design shear resistance per shear plane for an individual fastener, which is calculated as **Fv,Rd = αv fub A / γM2**. According to Table 3.4, this evaluation depends on the bolt's ultimate tensile strength and its cross-sectional area. Specifically, if the shear plane passes through the threaded portion of the bolt, the area **A** must be taken as the tensile stress area **A_s**. This follows EN 1993-1-8, Cl. 6.4.2 (Bolted joints) with supporting classification guidance from Cl. 6.2.6.4.1 (Unstiffened column flange, bolted connection). --- **References:**
1. EN 1993-1-8, Cl. 6.4.2 — Bolted joints

(1) A beam-to-column joint in which the design moment resistance of the joint $M_{j,Rd}$ is governed by the design resistance of the column web panel in shear, may be assumed to have adequate rotation capacity for plastic global analysis, provided that $d_{wc}/t_w \leq 69\epsilon$.

(2) A joint with either a bolted end-plate or angle flange cleat connection may be assumed to have sufficient rotation capacity for plastic analysis, provided that both of the following conditions are satisfied:

a) the design moment resistance of the joint is governed by the design resistance of either:

- the column flange in bending or
- the beam end-plate or tension flange cleat in bending.

b) the thickness $t$ of either the column flange or the beam end-plate or tension flange cleat (not necessarily the same basic component as in (a)) satisfies:

$$t \leq 0.36d\sqrt{f_{ub}/fy}$$

... (6.32)

where:

$$fy$$ is the yield strength of the relevant basic component;

$$d$$ is the nominal diameter of the bolt;

$$f_{ub}$$ is the ultimate tensile strength of the bolt material.

(3) A joint with a bolted connection in which the design moment resistance $M_{j,Rd}$ is governed by the design resistance of its bolts in shear, should not be assumed to have sufficient rotation capacity for plastic global analysis.

2. EN 1993-1-8, Cl. 6.2.6.4.1 — Unstiffened column flange, bolted connection

(1) The design resistance and failure mode of an unstiffened column flange in tranverse bending, together with the associated bolts in tension, should be taken as similar to those of an equivalent T-stub flange, see 6.2.4, for both:

- each individual bolt-row required to resist tension;
- each group of bolt-rows required to resist tension.

(2) The dimensions $e_{min}$ and $m$ for use in 6.2.4 should be determined from Figure 6.8.

(3) The effective length of equivalent T-stub flange should be determined for the individual bolt-rows and the bolt-group in accordance with 6.2.4.2 from the values given for each bolt-row in Table 6.4.

BS EN 1993-1-8:2005
EN 1993-1-8:2005 (E)

a) Welded end-plate narrower than column flange.

b) Welded end-plate wider than column flange.

c) Angle flange cleats.

a) the design moment resistance of the joint is governed by the design resistance of either:

- the beam end-plate or tension flange cleat in bending.

$$t \leq 0.36d\sqrt{f_{ub}/fy}$$

response.answer:
The design shear resistance is **$F_{v,Rd} = \frac{\alpha_v f_{ub} A}{\gamma_{M2}}$**, which must exceed the applied shear force. This calculation uses the bolt's tensile stress area $A_s$ if the shear plane passes through the threads, as per Table 3.4. You must also verify the bearing resistance **$F_{b,Rd} = 2.5 \alpha_b k_t fu d t / \gamma_{M2}$** to ensure the connected plate material is adequate. This follows EN 1993-1-3, Cl. Table 8.4 (Design resistances for bolts) with supporting guidance from EN 1993-1-8, Cl. Table 3.4 (Design resistance for individual fasteners subjected to shear and/or tension). --- **References:**
1. EN 1993-1-3, Table 8.4 — Design resistances for bolts

Bolts loaded in shear:

Bearing resistance:

$$F_{\text{b,Rd}} = 2.5 \alpha_b k_t fu d t / \gamma_{M2}$$ with $\alpha_b$ is the smallest of 1,0 or $e_1$ / (3d) and $k_t = (0.8 t + 1.5) / 2.5$ for 0,75 mm ≤ t ≤ 1,25 mm; $k_t = 1.0$ for t > 1,25 mm

Net-section resistance:

$$F_{\text{n,Rd}} = (1 + 3 r (d_o / u - 0.3)) A_{\text{net}} fu / \gamma_{M2}$$ but $F_{\text{n,Rd}} \leq A_{\text{net}} fu / \gamma_{M2}$ with:

$$r = [\text{number of bolts at the cross-section}] / [\text{total number of bolts in the connection}]$$

$$u = 2 e_2$$ but $u \leq p_2$

Shear resistance:

- for strength grades 4.6, 5.6 and 8.8:

$$F_{\text{v,Rd}} = 0.6 f_{\text{ub}} A_s / \gamma_{M2}$$

- for strength grades 4.8, 5.8, 6.8 and 10.9:

$$F_{\text{v,Rd}} = 0.5 f_{\text{ub}} A_s / \gamma_{M2}$$

Conditions:

$$F_{\text{v,Rd}} \geq 1.2 \Sigma F_{\text{b,Rd}} \text{ or } \Sigma F_{\text{v,Rd}} \geq 1.2 F_{\text{n,Rd}}$$

Bolts loaded in tension:

Pull-through resistance: Pull-through resistance $F_{\text{p,Rd}}$ to be determined by testing $^{*4}$.

Pull-out resistance: Not relevant for bolts.

Tension resistance: $F_{\text{t,Rd}} = 0.9 f_{\text{ub}} A_s / \gamma_{M2}$

Conditions:

$$F_{\text{t,Rd}} \geq \Sigma F_{\text{p,Rd}}$$

Range of validity:

$$e_1 \geq 1.0 d_o \quad p_1 \geq 3 d_o \quad 0.75 \text{ mm} \leq t < 3 \text{ mm}$$
Minimum bolt size: M6
$$e_2 \geq 1.5 d_o \quad p_2 \geq 3 d_o$$
Str …

2. EN 1993-1-8, Table 3.4 — Design resistance for individual fasteners subjected to shear and/or tension

| Failure mode | Bolts | Rivets |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Shear resistance per shear plane | $F_{v,Rd} = \frac{\alpha_v f_{ub} A}{\gamma_M 2}$ |
| - where the shear plane passes through the threaded portion of the bolt ($A$ is the tensile stress area of the bolt $A_s$): |
| - for classes 4.6, 5.6 and 8.8: $\alpha_v = 0, 6$ |
| - for classes 4.8, 5.8, 6.8 and 10.9: $\alpha_v = 0, 5$ |
| - where the shear plane passes through the unthreaded portion of the bolt ($A$ is the gross cross section of the bolt): $\alpha_v = 0, 6$ | $F_{v,Rd} = \frac{0,6 f_{ur} A_0}{\gamma_M 2}$ |

Bearing resistance 1), 2), 3)

$$F_{b,Rd} = \frac{k_1 \alpha_b fu dt}{\gamma_M 2}$$

where $\alpha_b$ is the smallest of $\alpha_d$; $\frac{f_{ub}}{fu}$ or 1,0;

in the direction of load transfer:

- for end bolts: $\alpha_d = \frac{e_1}{3d_0}$; for inner bolts: $\alpha_d = \frac{p_1}{3d_0} - \frac{1}{4}$ perpendicular to the direction of load transfer:

$$F_{ac2} -$$ for edge bolts: $k_1$ is the smallest of $2,8 \frac{e_2}{d_0} - 1,7,1,4 \frac{p_2}{d_0} - 1,7$ and $2,5 \frac{p_2}{d_0} - 1,7$ or 2,5

- for inner bolts: $k_1$ is the smallest of $1,4 \frac{p_2}{d_0} - 1,7$ or 2,5

Tension resistance 2)

$$F_{t,Rd} = \frac{k_2 f_{ub} A_s}{\gamma_M 2}$$

where $k_2 = 0,63$ for countersunk bolt, otherwise $k_2 = 0,9$.

Punching shear resistance

$$B_{p,Rd} = 0,6 \pi d_m t_p fu / \gamma_M 2$$

No check needed

C …

3. EN 1993-1-8, Cl. 3.4.1 — Shear connections

(1) Bolted connections loaded in shear should be designed as one of the following:

a) Category A: Bearing type
In this category bolts from class 4.6 up to and including class 10.9 should be used. No preloading and special provisions for contact surfaces are required. The design ultimate shear load should not exceed the design shear resistance, obtained from 3.6, nor the design bearing resistance, obtained from 3.6 and 3.7.

b) Category B: Slip-resistant at serviceability limit state
In this category preloaded bolts in accordance with 3.1.2(1) should be used. Slip should not occur at the serviceability limit state. The design serviceability shear load should not exceed the design slip resistance, obtained from 3.9. The design ultimate shear load should not exceed the design shear resistance, obtained from 3.6, nor the design bearing resistance, obtained from 3.6 and 3.7.

c) Category C: Slip-resistant at ultimate limit state
In this category preloaded bolts in accordance with 3.1.2(1) should be used. Slip should not occur at the ultimate limit state. The design ultimate shear load should not exceed the design slip resistance, obtained from 3.9, nor the design bearing resistance, obtained from 3.6 and 3.7. In addition for a connection in tension, the design plastic resistance of the net cross-section at bolt holes $N_{\text{net,Rd}}$, (see 6.2 of EN 1993-1-1), should be checked, …

4. EN 1993-1-2, D.1 — Bolted joints

(1) Net-section failure at fastener holes need not be considered, provided that there is a fastener in each hole, because the steel temperature is lower at joints due to the presence of additional material.

D1.1 Design Resistance of Bolts in Shear

D1.1.1 Category A: Bearing Type

(1) The fire design resistance of bolts loaded in shear should be determined from:

$$F_{v,t,Rd} = F_{v,Rd} k_{b,\theta} \frac{\gamma_{M2}}{\gamma_{M,fi}}$$

(D.1)

where
- $k_{b,\theta}$ is the reduction factor determined for the appropriate bolt temperature from Table D.1;
- $F_{v,Rd}$ is the design shear resistance of the bolt per shear plane calculated assuming that the shear plane passes through the threads of the bolt (table 3.4 of EN 1993-1-8);
- $\gamma_{M2}$ is the partial factor at normal temperature;
- $\gamma_{M,fi}$ is the partial factor for fire conditions.

(2) The design bearing resistance of bolts in fire should be determined from:

$$F_{b,t,Rd} = F_{b,Rd} k_{b,\theta} \frac{\gamma_{M2}}{\gamma_{M,fi}}$$

(D.2)

where
- $F_{b,Rd}$ is determined from table 3.4 EN1993-1.8;
- $k_{b,\theta}$ is the reduction factor determined for the appropriate bolt temperature from Table D.1

D1.1.2 Category B: Slip resistance at serviceability and category C Slip resistance at ultimate state

(1) Slip resistant joints should be considered as having slipped in fire and the resistance of a single bolt s …

5. EN 1993-1-4, Cl. 6.2 — Bolted connections

(1) Bearing strength should be calculated by replacing $fu$ by a reduced value $f_{u,red}$ given by:

$$f_{u,red} = 0.5fy + 0.6fu \quad \text{but} \leq fu$$

(2) Stainless steel bolts in shear to EN ISO 3506 property classes 50, 70 and 80 should be treated like bolts grades 4.6, 5.6 and 8.8.

(3) The shear resistance of a bolt, $F_{v,Rd}$ should be determined from the following:

$$F_{v,Rd} = \frac{\alpha f_{ub} A}{\gamma_{M2}}$$

where

$A$ is the gross cross-section area of the bolt (if the shear plane passes through unthreaded portion of the bolt); or the tensile stress area of the bolt (if the shear plane passes through the threaded portion of the bolt);

$f_{ub}$ is the ultimate tensile strength of the bolt, see Table 2.2.

NOTE: The value of $\alpha$ may be defined in the National Annex. The recommended values are:

- if the shear plane passes through unthreaded portion of the bolt, $\alpha = 0,6$
- if the shear plane passes through the threaded portion of the bolt, $\alpha = 0,5$

6. EN 1993-1-8, Cl. 3.9.2 — Combined tension and shear

(1) If a slip-resistant connection is subjected to an applied tensile force, $F_{t,Ed}$ or $F_{t,Ed,ser}$, in addition to the shear force, $F_{v,Ed}$ or $F_{v,Ed,ser}$, tending to produce slip, the design slip resistance per bolt should be taken as follows:

$$F_{s,Rd,ser} = \frac{k_s n \mu (F_{p,C} - 0.8 F_{t,Ed,ser})}{\gamma_{M3,ser}}$$ ... (3.8a)

for a category B connection:

$$F_{s,Rd} = \frac{k_s n \mu (F_{p,C} - 0.8 F_{t,Ed})}{\gamma_{M3}}$$ ... (3.8b)

(2) If, in a moment connection, a contact force on the compression side counterbalances the applied tensile force no reduction in slip resistance is required.
